Why Sellers Choose DealBridge

Free Business Valuation

Get a professional, data-driven valuation of your business at no cost. Know what you're worth before you list.

$0 Commission

We don't earn a cent until you close. No upfront fees, no listing fees — just results.

90-Day Close Guarantee

Our network of qualified buyers moves fast. Most listings see serious interest within two weeks.

M&A Advisors in Your Corner

Our deal room connects you with experienced M&A professionals who guide you from LOI to close.

Timing

Is Now the Right Time to Sell?

Most business owners wait too long. The best time to sell is when your business is performing well — not when you're burned out or the market shifts. Here are the signals that indicate it's time to start the conversation.

You've hit a growth plateau

Revenue has been flat for 12–24 months and you'd need significant capital investment to reach the next level. A strategic buyer can provide that — and pay you for the foundation you built.

You're approaching retirement or a life change

Planning a sale 2–3 years before your target exit date gives you time to maximize value, not just get out. Rushed exits leave money on the table.

A buyer has already approached you

Unsolicited offers are often well below market. Before responding, get an independent valuation so you know what a fair offer looks like.

You're tired of running it

Owner fatigue is real. If your best years of energy are behind the business, buyers can see it in the numbers. Selling while revenue is healthy protects your outcome.

Valuation 101

How We Value Your Business

Most small business sales are priced using a multiple of your Seller's Discretionary Earnings (SDE) — the total financial benefit a working owner receives from the business annually. Here's how it breaks down.

Step 1 — Calculate SDE

We start with your net profit and add back owner salary, benefits, depreciation, and any one-time expenses. SDE shows a buyer exactly what they could earn running the business.

Step 2 — Apply an Industry Multiple

Multiples typically range from 2x–5x SDE depending on your industry, business size, growth trajectory, and risk profile. A home services company with recurring contracts commands a higher multiple than a one-time-project business.

Step 3 — Adjust for Assets & Risk

Equipment, real estate, inventory, and intellectual property add value. Concentration risk (one client = 40% of revenue) reduces it. We document all of this in your written valuation report.

Step 4 — Compare to Market

We benchmark your business against recently closed comparable sales so you know where you stand in the current market — not last year's data.

Buyer Mindset

What Buyers Are Really Looking For

Understanding what makes a business attractive to buyers helps you prepare long before listing. These six factors drive premium valuations.

🔄

Recurring Revenue

Contracts, subscriptions, or loyal repeat customers reduce buyer risk and justify higher multiples.

👥

A Team That Runs It

Businesses that don't depend on the owner day-to-day are far more attractive and command higher prices.

📈

Consistent Growth

3 years of stable or growing revenue signals that the business model works independent of any single client.

📋

Clean Financials

Organized books, filed taxes, and clear profit & loss statements eliminate buyer uncertainty and speed up closing.

🏆

Strong Brand & Reputation

Reviews, referrals, and a recognizable name in the community transfer real value to a new owner.

⚙️

Documented Processes

SOPs, employee handbooks, and operational systems show buyers the business can run without you.

Step by Step

The DealBridge Selling Process

We've guided hundreds of business owners through this process. Here's exactly what to expect from your first call to closing day.

01

Confidential Consultation

Week 1

You speak with a DealBridge advisor — no paperwork, no commitment. We listen to your goals, timeline, and concerns. Everything is fully confidential at this stage.

02

Business Valuation

Week 1–2

We review your last 3 years of financials and deliver a written valuation report within 3 business days. You'll know your number before anyone else does.

03

Listing & Buyer Outreach

Week 2–4

We create a confidential business profile and present it to our vetted buyer network. Prospective buyers sign an NDA before they see any identifying details about your business.

04

Buyer Meetings & LOI

Week 4–8

We vet buyer interest, facilitate introductions, and manage offers. Our advisors help you evaluate Letters of Intent and negotiate deal structure — price, earnouts, seller financing, and transition terms.

05

Due Diligence

Week 8–14

Buyers review your financials, contracts, and operations. We manage the process and protect you from fishing expeditions. Our deal room keeps communication organized and documented.

06

Closing & Transition

Week 14–20

Purchase agreements are signed, funds are transferred, and you hand over the keys. We coordinate with attorneys, accountants, and lenders to get you to closing day with no surprises.

Seller Mistakes

5 Costly Mistakes Business Owners Make When Selling

Most business owners sell once in their lifetime. These are the mistakes we see most often — and how to avoid them.

1

Waiting Until Revenue Drops

Selling from a position of strength gives you leverage. Selling out of desperation gives the buyer all the cards.

2

Not Knowing Your Number

Accepting the first offer without getting an independent valuation is the single most expensive mistake sellers make.

3

Telling Employees or Customers Too Soon

Premature disclosure causes key staff to job hunt and customers to look elsewhere. Keep it confidential until closing.

4

Using the Wrong Broker

Many business brokers take 10–12% commission and move slowly. DealBridge charges zero commission and moves with urgency — because we only earn when you close.

5

Neglecting the Business During the Sale

Buyers perform due diligence on real-time performance. If revenue dips during the sale process, the deal can fall apart or reprice. Keep running the business.

Seller FAQ

Questions from Sellers

Answers to the most common concerns we hear from business owners considering a sale.

Not unless you tell them. We keep all listings confidential. Buyers sign a Non-Disclosure Agreement before receiving any identifying information. The sale can remain completely private until you choose to disclose it — typically at or after closing.

Most DealBridge listings receive qualified buyer interest within 2–4 weeks. Full closings typically take 90–180 days depending on deal complexity, buyer financing, and due diligence. Simpler asset sales can close faster. SBA-financed deals take longer due to bank processing.

Three years is the standard. However, we can work with 2 years of records plus strong current-year performance. The more documentation you have, the stronger your position and the higher the multiple you can command.

This is extremely common and often preferred by buyers. A transition period of 90 days to 2 years, sometimes with an earnout component, helps the new owner succeed and maximizes your total payout.

We calculate your Seller's Discretionary Earnings (SDE) — net profit plus owner compensation, benefits, and add-backs — then apply an industry-appropriate multiple (typically 2x–5x). We compare to recent comparable sales and adjust for assets, contracts, and risk factors.

Seller financing means you receive a portion of the sale price over time rather than all at closing. It can make your business more attractive to buyers who don't have full capital. It's not required, but offering it can increase your sale price and close the deal faster.

In an asset sale, the buyer purchases the business's assets (equipment, client lists, contracts) but not its legal entity. In a stock sale, they buy the entire company including liabilities. Asset sales are more common for small businesses. We'll help you understand the tax implications of each.

Start with What Your Business Is Worth

A free, confidential valuation takes 3 business days and costs nothing. It's the smartest first step any seller can take.
